chunks.heading: Output Mode
chunks.snippet:

All static? ──> output: 'static' (default, no adapter)
All dynamic? ──> output: 'server' + adapter
Mix? ──> output: 'hybrid' + adapter
  Opt into SSR (hybrid): export const prerender = false
  Opt

chunks.title: Astro Framework Cheatsheet
chunks.heading: Hydration Directive
chunks.snippet:
```
Needs interactivity?
├─ No ──> No directive (zero JS)
└─ Yes ──> Above fold + critical? ──> client:load
            Above fold + non-critical? ──> client:idle
            Below fold? ──> client:vi

chunks.title: Astro Framework Cheatsheet
chunks.heading: Server Island vs Client Island
chunks.snippet:
```
Needs server data (cookies, DB, personalization)? ──> server:defer + slot="fallback"
Needs browser interactivity? ──> client:* directive
Neither? ──> Static .astro component
```

chunks.heading: Critical "NEVER Do" List
chunks.snippet:
- **NEVER** access `window`/`document` in `.astro` frontmatter (server-only)
- **NEVER** use `client:load` on everything — defeats islands architecture
- **NEVER** use `client:only` without the framew

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: Client Directives
chunks.snippet: Client directives control how UI framework components (React, Vue, Svelte, etc.) are hydrated on the client.

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: Overview
chunks.snippet:
By default, UI framework components are **not hydrated** - they render to static HTML. Client directives tell Astro when and how to hydrate them.

```astro
<!-- Static - no JavaScript shipped -->
<Rea

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: client:load
chunks.snippet:
Hydrate immediately when the page loads. Use for above-the-fold interactive content.

```astro
<InteractiveCounter client:load />
<NavigationMenu client:load />
```

**Use when:**
- Component is immed

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: client:idle
chunks.snippet:
Hydrate when the browser is idle (using `requestIdleCallback`). Good for lower-priority interactivity.

```astro
<SidebarWidget client:idle />
<FeedbackForm client:idle />
```

**Use when:**
- Compone

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: client:visible
chunks.snippet:
Hydrate when the component enters the viewport. Uses Intersection Observer.

```astro
<CommentsSection client:visible />
<ImageCarousel client:visible />
<FooterNewsletter client:visible />
```

**Use

chunks.title: Client Directives
chunks.heading: client:media

```astro
<!-- Only hydrate on desktop -->
<DesktopOnlyChart client:media="(min-width: 768px)" />

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Deepening
chunks.snippet: How to deepen a cluster of shallow modules safely, given its dependencies. Assumes the vocabulary in [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) — **module**, **interface**, **seam**, **adapter**.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Dependency categories
chunks.snippet: When assessing a candidate for deepening, classify its dependencies. The category determines how the deepened module is tested across its seam.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 1. In-process
chunks.snippet: Pure computation, in-memory state, no I/O. Always deepenable — merge the modules and test through the new interface directly. No adapter needed.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 2. Local-substitutable
chunks.snippet: Dependencies that have local test stand-ins (PGLite for Postgres, in-memory filesystem). Deepenable if the stand-in exists. The deepened module is tested with the stand-in running in the test suite. T

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 3. Remote but owned (Ports & Adapters)
chunks.snippet: Your own services across a network boundary (microservices, internal APIs). Define a **port** (interface) at the seam. The deep module owns the logic; the transport is injected as an **adapter**. Test

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 4. True external (Mock)
chunks.snippet: Third-party services (Stripe, Twilio, etc.) you don't control. The deepened module takes the external dependency as an injected port; tests provide a mock adapter.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Seam discipline
chunks.snippet: - **One adapter means a hypothetical seam. Two adapters means a real one.** Don't introduce a port unless at least two adapters are justified (typically production + test). A single-adapter seam is ju

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Testing strategy: replace, don't layer
chunks.snippet:
- Old unit tests on shallow modules become waste once tests at the deepened module's interface exist — delete them.
- Write new tests at the deepened module's interface. The **interface is the test su

chunks.heading: Design It Twice

chunks.title: Design It Twice

chunks.snippet:
Before spawning sub-agents, write a user-facing explanation of the problem space for the chosen candidate:

- The constraints any new interface would need to satisfy
- The dependencies it would rely o

chunks.title: Design It Twice
chunks.heading: 2. Spawn sub-agents
chunks.snippet:
Spawn 3+ sub-agents in parallel using the Agent tool. Each must produce a **radically different** interface for the deepened module.

Prompt each sub-agent with a separate technical brief (file paths,

chunks.title: Design It Twice
chunks.heading: 3. Present and compare

chunks.title: Domain Modeling

Most repos have a single context:

If a `CONTEXT-MAP.md`

chunks.title: Domain Modeling
chunks.heading: Challenge against the glossary
chunks.snippet: When the user uses a term that conflicts with the existing language in `CONTEXT.md`, call it out immediately. "Your glossary defines 'cancellation' as X, but you seem to mean Y — which is it?"

chunks.title: Domain Modeling
chunks.heading: Sharpen fuzzy language
chunks.snippet: When the user uses vague or overloaded terms, propose a precise canonical term. "You're saying 'account' — do you mean the Customer or the User? Those are different things."

chunks.title: Domain Modeling
chunks.heading: Discuss concrete scenarios
chunks.snippet: When domain relationships are being discussed, stress-test them with specific scenarios. Invent scenarios that probe edge cases and force the user to be precise about the boundaries between concepts.

chunks.title: Domain Modeling
chunks.heading: Cross-reference with code
chunks.snippet: When the user states how something works, check whether the code agrees. If you find a contradiction, surface it: "Your code cancels entire Orders, but you just said partial cancellation is possible —

chunks.title: Domain Modeling
chunks.heading: Update CONTEXT.md inline
chunks.snippet:
When a term is resolved, update `CONTEXT.md` right there. Don't batch these up — capture them as they happen. Use the format in [CONTEXT-FORMAT.md](./CONTEXT-FORMAT.md).

`CONTEXT.md` should be totall

chunks.title: HTML Report Format

chunks.snippet:
The diagrams carry the weight. Prose is sparse, plain, and uses the glossary terms (from the `/codebase-design` skill) without ceremony.

Each candidate is one `<article>`:

- **Title** — short, names

chunks.title: HTML Report Format

chunks.snippet: Pick the pattern that fits the candidate. Mix them. Don't make every diagram look the same — variety is part of the point.

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Mermaid graph (the workhorse for dependencies / call flow)
chunks.snippet: Use a Mermaid `flowchart` or `graph` when the point is "X calls Y calls Z, and look at the mess." Wrap it in a Tailwind-styled card so it doesn't feel parachuted in. Style with classDef to colour leak

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Hand-built boxes-and-arrows (when Mermaid's layout fights you)
chunks.snippet: Modules as `<div>`s with borders and labels. Arrows as inline SVG `<line>` or `<path>` elements positioned absolutely over a relative container. Reach for this when you want the "after" diagram to fee

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Cross-section (good for layered shallowness)
chunks.snippet: Stack horizontal bands (`h-12 border-l-4`) to show layers a call passes through. Before: 6 thin layers each doing nothing. After: 1 thick band labelled with the consolidated responsibility.

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Mass diagram (good for "interface as wide as implementation")
chunks.snippet: Two rectangles per module — one for interface surface area, one for implementation. Before: interface rectangle is nearly as tall as the implementation rectangle (shallow). After: interface rectangle

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Call-graph collapse
chunks.snippet: Before: a tree of function calls rendered as nested boxes. After: the same tree collapsed into one box, with the now-internal calls shown faded inside it.

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Style guidance
chunks.snippet:
- Lean editorial, not corporate-dashboard. Generous whitespace. Serif optional for headings (`font-serif` works well with stone/slate).
- Colour sparingly: one accent (emerald or indigo) plus red for

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Top recommendation section
chunks.snippet: One larger card. Candidate name, one sentence on why, anchor link to its card. That's it.

chunks.title: HTML Report Format
chunks.heading: Tone
chunks.snippet:
Plain English, concise — but the architectural nouns and verbs come straight from the `/codebase-design` skill. Concision is not an excuse to drift.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.snippet:
---
name: planning-with-files
description: This skill should be used when starting complex multi-step tasks, research projects, or any task requiring >5 tool calls. Implements Manus-style file-based p

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Planning with Files
chunks.snippet: Work like Manus: Use persistent markdown files as your "working memory on disk."

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: The Core Pattern
chunks.snippet:
```
Context Window = RAM (volatile, limited)
Filesystem = Disk (persistent, unlimited)

Anything important gets written to disk.
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Quick Start
chunks.snippet:
Before ANY complex task:

1. **Create `task_plan.md`** - Use the template below
2. **Create `findings.md`** - For research and discoveries
3. **Create `progress.md`** - For session logging
4. **Re-rea

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: File Purposes
chunks.snippet:
| File | Purpose | When to Update |
|------|---------|----------------|
| `task_plan.md` | Phases, progress, decisions | After each phase |
| `findings.md` | Research, discoveries | After ANY discover

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 1. Create Plan First
chunks.snippet: Never start a complex task without `task_plan.md`. Non-negotiable.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 2. The 2-Action Rule
chunks.snippet:
> "After every 2 view/browser/search operations, IMMEDIATELY save key findings to text files."

This prevents visual/multimodal information from being lost.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 3. Read Before Decide
chunks.snippet: Before major decisions, read the plan file. This keeps goals in your attention window.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 4. Update After Act
chunks.snippet:
After completing any phase:

- Mark phase status: `in_progress` -> `complete`
- Log any errors encountered
- Note files created/modified

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 5. Log ALL Errors
chunks.snippet:
Every error goes in the plan file. This builds knowledge and prevents repetition.

```markdown

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Errors Encountered
chunks.snippet:
| Error | Attempt | Resolution |
|-------|---------|------------|
| FileNotFoundError | 1 | Created default config |
| API timeout | 2 | Added retry logic |
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 6. Never Repeat Failures
chunks.snippet:
```
if action_failed:
    next_action != same_action
```

Track what you tried. Mutate the approach.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: The 3-Strike Error Protocol
chunks.snippet:
```
ATTEMPT 1: Diagnose & Fix
  -> Read error carefully
  -> Identify root cause
  -> Apply targeted fix

ATTEMPT 2: Alternative Approach
  -> Same error? Try different method
  -> Different tool? Dif

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Read vs Write Decision Matrix
chunks.snippet:
| Situation | Action | Reason |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Just wrote a file | DON'T read | Content still in context |
| Viewed image/PDF | Write findings NOW | Multimodal -> text before lost |

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: The 5-Question Reboot Test
chunks.snippet:
If you can answer these, your context management is solid:

| Question | Answer Source |
|----------|---------------|
| Where am I? | Current phase in task_plan.md |
| Where am I going? | Remaining ph

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: task_plan.md Template
chunks.snippet: ```markdown

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Goal
chunks.snippet: [Clear statement of what we're trying to accomplish]

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Phase 1: [Name]
chunks.snippet:
- Status: pending | in_progress | complete
- Tasks:
  - [ ] Task 1
  - [ ] Task 2

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Phase 2: [Name]
chunks.snippet:
- Status: pending
- Tasks:
  - [ ] Task 1

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Files Modified
chunks.snippet:
- [file1.py] - [what changed]
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: findings.md Template
chunks.snippet: ```markdown

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: [Topic 1]
chunks.snippet:
- Key insight 1
- Key insight 2
- Source: [where found]

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: [Topic 2]
chunks.snippet:
- Finding 1
- Finding 2
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: progress.md Template
chunks.snippet: ```markdown

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: [Date/Time]
chunks.snippet:
- Started: [task]
- Completed: [what]
- Blocked on: [if any]
- Next: [what's next]
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: When to Use This Pattern
chunks.snippet:
**Use for:**

- Multi-step tasks (3+ steps)
- Research tasks
- Building/creating projects
- Tasks spanning many tool calls
- Anything requiring organization

**Skip for:**

- Simple questions
- Single

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Anti-Patterns
chunks.snippet:
| Don't | Do Instead |
|-------|------------|
| Use TodoWrite for persistence | Create task_plan.md file |
| State goals once and forget | Re-read plan before decisions |
| Hide errors and retry silen

`

chunks.title: View Transitions
chunks.heading: How It Works
chunks.snippet:
With `<ClientRouter />` enabled:
1. User clicks a link
2. Astro intercepts the navigation
3. New page content is fetched
4. Transitions animate between old and new content
5. Browser history is update

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Deepening
chunks.snippet: How to deepen a cluster of shallow modules safely, given its dependencies. Assumes the vocabulary in [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) — **module**, **interface**, **seam**, **adapter**.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Dependency categories
chunks.snippet: When assessing a candidate for deepening, classify its dependencies. The category determines how the deepened module is tested across its seam.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 1. In-process
chunks.snippet: Pure computation, in-memory state, no I/O. Always deepenable — merge the modules and test through the new interface directly. No adapter needed.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 2. Local-substitutable
chunks.snippet: Dependencies that have local test stand-ins (PGLite for Postgres, in-memory filesystem). Deepenable if the stand-in exists. The deepened module is tested with the stand-in running in the test suite. T

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 3. Remote but owned (Ports & Adapters)
chunks.snippet: Your own services across a network boundary (microservices, internal APIs). Define a **port** (interface) at the seam. The deep module owns the logic; the transport is injected as an **adapter**. Test

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: 4. True external (Mock)
chunks.snippet: Third-party services (Stripe, Twilio, etc.) you don't control. The deepened module takes the external dependency as an injected port; tests provide a mock adapter.

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Seam discipline
chunks.snippet: - **One adapter means a hypothetical seam. Two adapters means a real one.** Don't introduce a port unless at least two adapters are justified (typically production + test). A single-adapter seam is ju

chunks.title: Deepening
chunks.heading: Testing strategy: replace, don't layer
chunks.snippet:
- Old unit tests on shallow modules become waste once tests at the deepened module's interface exist — delete them.
- Write new tests at the deepened module's interface. The **interface is the test su

chunks.title: Design It Twice
chunks.heading: Design It Twice
chunks.snippet: When the user wants to explore alternative interfaces for a chosen deepening candidate, use this parallel sub-agent pattern. Based on "Design It Twice" (Ousterhout) — your first idea is unlikely to be

chunks.title: Design It Twice
chunks.heading: 1. Frame the problem space
chunks.snippet:
Before spawning sub-agents, write a user-facing explanation of the problem space for the chosen candidate:

- The constraints any new interface would need to satisfy
- The dependencies it would rely o

chunks.title: Design It Twice
chunks.heading: 2. Spawn sub-agents
chunks.snippet:
Spawn 3+ sub-agents in parallel using the Agent tool. Each must produce a **radically different** interface for the deepened module.

Prompt each sub-agent with a separate technical brief (file paths,

chunks.title: Debugging and Error Recovery
chunks.heading: Step 1: Reproduce
chunks.snippet:
Make the failure happen reliably. If you can't reproduce it, you can't fix it with confidence.

chunks.title: Debugging and Error Recovery

git bisect good <known-good-sha> # This commit worked

chunks.title: Planning with Files

chunks.snippet: Work like Manus: Use persistent markdown files as your "working memory on disk."

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: The Core Pattern
chunks.snippet:
```
Context Window = RAM (volatile, limited)
Filesystem = Disk (persistent, unlimited)

Anything important gets written to disk.
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Quick Start
chunks.snippet:
Before ANY complex task:

1. **Create `task_plan.md`** - Use the template below
2. **Create `findings.md`** - For research and discoveries
3. **Create `progress.md`** - For session logging
4. **Re-rea

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: File Purposes
chunks.snippet:
| File | Purpose | When to Update |
|------|---------|----------------|
| `task_plan.md` | Phases, progress, decisions | After each phase |
| `findings.md` | Research, discoveries | After ANY discover

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 1. Create Plan First
chunks.snippet: Never start a complex task without `task_plan.md`. Non-negotiable.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 2. The 2-Action Rule
chunks.snippet:
> "After every 2 view/browser/search operations, IMMEDIATELY save key findings to text files."

This prevents visual/multimodal information from being lost.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 3. Read Before Decide
chunks.snippet: Before major decisions, read the plan file. This keeps goals in your attention window.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 4. Update After Act
chunks.snippet:
After completing any phase:

- Mark phase status: `in_progress` -> `complete`
- Log any errors encountered
- Note files created/modified

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 5. Log ALL Errors
chunks.snippet:
Every error goes in the plan file. This builds knowledge and prevents repetition.

```markdown

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: Errors Encountered
chunks.snippet:
| Error | Attempt | Resolution |
|-------|---------|------------|
| FileNotFoundError | 1 | Created default config |
| API timeout | 2 | Added retry logic |
```

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: 6. Never Repeat Failures
chunks.snippet:
```
if action_failed:
    next_action != same_action
```

Track what you tried. Mutate the approach.

chunks.title: Planning with Files
chunks.heading: The 3-Strike Error Protocol
chunks.snippet:
```
ATTEMPT 1: Diagnose & Fix
  -> Read error carefully
  -> Identify root cause
  -> Apply targeted fix

ATTEMPT 2: Alternative Approach
  -> Same error? Try different method
  -> Different tool? Dif

|-----------|--------|--------|
| Just wrote a file | DON'T read | Content still in context |
| Viewed image/PDF | Write findings NOW | Multimodal -> text before lost |

**Use for:**

- Building/creating projects

- Anything requiring organization

**Skip for:**

- Simple questions
- Single

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.snippet:
---
name: receiving-code-review
area: review
description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - r

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Overview
chunks.snippet:
Code review requires technical evaluation, not emotional performance.

**Core principle:** Verify before implementing. Ask before assuming. Technical correctness over social comfort.

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: The Response Pattern
chunks.snippet:
```
WHEN receiving code review feedback:

1. READ: Complete feedback without reacting
2. UNDERSTAND: Restate requirement in own words (or ask)
3. VERIFY: Check against codebase reality
4. EVALUATE: Te

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Forbidden Responses
chunks.snippet:
**NEVER:**
- "You're absolutely right!" (explicit instruction-file violation)
- "Great point!" / "Excellent feedback!" (performative)
- "Let me implement that now" (before verification)

**INSTEAD:**

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Handling Unclear Feedback
chunks.snippet:
```
IF any item is unclear:
  STOP - do not implement anything yet
  ASK for clarification on unclear items

WHY: Items may be related. Partial understanding = wrong implementation.
```

**Example:**

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: From your human partner
chunks.snippet:
- **Trusted** - implement after understanding
- **Still ask** if scope unclear
- **No performative agreement**
- **Skip to action** or technical acknowledgment

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: From External Reviewers
chunks.snippet:
```
BEFORE implementing:
  1. Check: Technically correct for THIS codebase?
  2. Check: Breaks existing functionality?
  3. Check: Reason for current implementation?
  4. Check: Works on all platforms

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: YAGNI Check for "Professional" Features
chunks.snippet:
```
IF reviewer suggests "implementing properly":
  grep codebase for actual usage

  IF unused: "This endpoint isn't called. Remove it (YAGNI)?"
  IF used: Then implement properly
```

**your human p

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Implementation Order
chunks.snippet:
```
FOR multi-item feedback:
  1. Clarify anything unclear FIRST
  2. Then implement in this order:
     - Blocking issues (breaks, security)
     - Simple fixes (typos, imports)
     - Complex fixes

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: When To Push Back
chunks.snippet:
Push back when:
- Suggestion breaks existing functionality
- Reviewer lacks full context
- Violates YAGNI (unused feature)
- Technically incorrect for this stack
- Legacy/compatibility reasons exist
-

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Acknowledging Correct Feedback
chunks.snippet:
When feedback IS correct:
```
✅ "Fixed. [Brief description of what changed]"
✅ "Good catch - [specific issue]. Fixed in [location]."
✅ [Just fix it and show in the code]

❌ "You're absolutely right!"

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Gracefully Correcting Your Pushback
chunks.snippet:
If you pushed back and were wrong:
```
✅ "You were right - I checked [X] and it does [Y]. Implementing now."
✅ "Verified this and you're correct. My initial understanding was wrong because [reason]. F

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Common Mistakes
chunks.snippet:
| Mistake | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| Performative agreement | State requirement or just act |
| Blind implementation | Verify against codebase first |
| Batch without testing | One at a time, test ea

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: Real Examples
chunks.snippet:
**Performative Agreement (Bad):**
```
Reviewer: "Remove legacy code"
❌ "You're absolutely right! Let me remove that..."
```

**Technical Verification (Good):**
```
Reviewer: "Remove legacy code"
✅ "Ch

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: GitHub Thread Replies
chunks.snippet: When replying to inline review comments on GitHub, reply in the comment thread (`gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments/{id}/replies`), not as a top-level PR comment.

chunks.title: Code Review Reception
chunks.heading: The Bottom Line
chunks.snippet:
**External feedback = suggestions to evaluate, not orders to follow.**

Verify. Question. Then implement.

No performative agreement. Technical rigor always.

chunks.title: Requesting Code Review
chunks.heading: Integration with Workflows
chunks.snippet:
**Subagent-Driven Development:**
- Review after EACH task
- Catch issues before they compound
- Fix before moving to next task

**Executing Plans:**
- Review after each task or at natural checkpoints

chunks.title: Requesting Code Review

**Never:**
- Skip review because "it's simple"
- Ignore Critical issues
- Proceed with unfixed Important issues

chunks.title: Verification Before Completion

**No shortcuts for verification.**

Run the command. Read the output. THEN claim the result.

This is non-negotiable.

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: Overview
chunks.snippet: LLMs respond to the same persuasion principles as humans. Understanding this psychology helps you design more effective skills - not to manipulate, but to ensure critical practices are followed even u

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 1. Authority
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Deference to expertise, credentials, or official sources.

**How it works in skills:**
- Imperative language: "YOU MUST", "Never", "Always"
- Non-negotiable framing: "No exceptions"
-

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 2. Commitment
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Consistency with prior actions, statements, or public declarations.

**How it works in skills:**
- Require announcements: "Announce skill usage"
- Force explicit choices: "Choose A, B,

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 3. Scarcity
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Urgency from time limits or limited availability.

**How it works in skills:**
- Time-bound requirements: "Before proceeding"
- Sequential dependencies: "Immediately after X"
- Prevent

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 4. Social Proof
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Conformity to what others do or what's considered normal.

**How it works in skills:**
- Universal patterns: "Every time", "Always"
- Failure modes: "X without Y = failure"
- Establish

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 5. Unity
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Shared identity, "we-ness", in-group belonging.

**How it works in skills:**
- Collaborative language: "our codebase", "we're colleagues"
- Shared goals: "we both want quality"

**When

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 6. Reciprocity
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Obligation to return benefits received.

**How it works:**
- Use sparingly - can feel manipulative
- Rarely needed in skills

**When to avoid:**
- Almost always (other principles more

chunks.title: Persuasion Principles for Skill Design
chunks.heading: 7. Liking
chunks.snippet:
**What it is:** Preference for cooperating with those we like.

**How it works:**
- **DON'T USE for compliance**
- Conflicts with honest feedback culture
- Creates sycophancy

**When to avoid:**
- Alw

chunks.snippet:

| Discipline-enforcing | Authority + Commitment + Social Proof | Liking, Reciprocity |
| Guidance/technique | Moderate Authority + Unity | Hea

chunks.snippet:

- "YOU MUST" removes decision fatigue
- Absolute language eliminates "is this an exception?" questions
- Explicit anti-rationalization counters close spec

chunks.snippet:

- Ensuring critical practices are followed
- Creating effective documentation
- Preventing predictable failures

**Illegitimate:**
- Manipulating for personal gain
- Creating false urg

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: primordial
chunks.snippet:
An **audio-reactive WebGL2 visual instrument** for live electronic-music gigs.

A musician opens a single HTTPS link, grants mic/line-in access, and the page
turns room audio (mic or line-in → FFT) in

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: What it is
chunks.snippet:
- **Static client-side app.** WebGL2 fullscreen-triangle renderer with FBO
  ping-pong post-processing; audio analysis on the main thread via
  `AnalyserNode`. No framework, no bundler required.
- **A

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: Quickstart (local)
chunks.snippet:
The mic requires a secure context, so serve over `localhost` (treated as
secure) or HTTPS — opening `index.html` via `file://` will not grant mic.

```bash

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: from the repo root
chunks.snippet: python3 -m http.server 8000

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: then open http://localhost:8000/  (localhost is a secure context → mic works)
chunks.snippet:
```

Click **Start** (a user gesture is required to resume the `AudioContext` on
iOS/Safari), pick an input device, and grant mic permission.

chunks.title: primordial
chunks.heading: Verify (no browser interaction needed)

budget enforced from day one (FBO render-scale, step

switching, reactivity tuning, sliders, device picker, plus an autonomous mode
for hands-off stretches. Versioned localStorage

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Problem
chunks.snippet:
There is no lightweight way to *discuss and creatively workshop visuals and
design* without going through the full build/deploy cycle. Today, trying a
visual idea means editing the shipped shader/app

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Goal
chunks.snippet:
A **throwaway sandbox loop**, separate from the shipped app, that supports the
full creative lifecycle — **mood/reference → (optional) research & trend scan →
new directions from scratch → refining a

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Success criteria
chunks.snippet:
- I can spin up a new experimental visual without touching `src/`.
- I can render it to a `~5s` looping `.webm` clip (and stills) and deliver it to
  the phone via `SendUserFile` — **no deploy, no mic

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Non-goals
chunks.snippet:
- **Not** live-mic preview, and **not** a deployed sandbox URL (the operator
  chose clips-in-chat; live/deploy paths are explicitly out of scope here).
- **Not** a GUI shader editor or node graph — a

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Architecture
chunks.snippet:
Four components. The data flow is: **discussion → sketch (GLSL+params) →
sandbox render (synthetic audio) → clip → chat → iterate → graduate.**

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: 1. `workshop/sketches/` — durable experiments
chunks.snippet:
Each sketch is self-contained and **committed to git** (tiny text files; they
are the creative history and must survive cloud wipes):

```
workshop/sketches/<name>/
├── <name>.frag.js     # experiment

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: 2. `workshop/sandbox.html` — the mount point
chunks.snippet:
A minimal page (analogous to `index.html` but disposable) that:

- Reads `?sketch=<name>` and loads that sketch's fragment shader.
- Reuses the **real** `src/gl/renderer.js`, `src/gl/passes.js`, and

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: 3. `tools/workshop/clip.mjs` — the recorder (`npm run clip -- <name>`)
chunks.snippet:
- Serves the repo and loads `workshop/sandbox.html?sketch=<name>` in headless
  Chromium via Playwright (already a devDep; reuses `tools/mcp/lib/browser.mjs`
  and the serving logic in `tools/mcp/lib/

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: 4. `/workshop` skill — drives the loop
chunks.snippet:
A new skill (`.claude/skills/visual-workshop/SKILL.md`, area `design`) that
encodes the method so it is repeatable:

1. **Mood/reference** — discuss in chat; collect references/palette/vibe words.

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Uniform contract (sketch ↔ app parity)
chunks.snippet:
The sandbox provides a sketch the exact uniforms the shipped slime pass receives
(from `src/gl/uniforms.js`), so a sketch is "just a fragment shader" against our
contract and graduation needs no rewir

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Synthetic audio model
chunks.snippet:
The sandbox synthesizes a deterministic "fake song" each frame instead of a mic
feed, producing both the band scalars and the 512×2 audio texture so audio
shaders behave as they would live:

- A `bpm`

chunks.title: Visual Workshop — design spec
chunks.heading: Research step (optional, opt-in)
chunks.snippet:
